comments: This paper proposes an optimization-based extension of the word-graph approach to Multi-Sentence Compression (MSC). Building on Filippova (2010) and Boudin & Morin (2013), the authors formalize an Integer Linear Program (ILP) that jointly optimizes path cohesion, rewards presence of document keywords (extracted via LDA) and frequent 3-grams, and enforces length and connectivity constraints. They report experiments on the Boudin & Morin French corpus (40 clusters), including automatic evaluation (ROUGE-1/2) and manual assessment (grammaticality and informativity), and show improvements over the two baselines. Strengths: - Clear motivation: addressing the limitation of selecting only the smallest cohesion paths by incorporating keyword and n-gram signals. - Formal mathematical model (variables, constraints, MTZ subtour elimination) and explicit objective combining cohesion and bonuses. - Empirical evaluation includes both automatic metrics and human judgments, and compares against established baselines. - Implementation details (tools and solver) are provided, and reported per-document runtimes are acceptable. Weaknesses and issues to address or clarify: - Dataset size and generality: the corpus is small (40 clusters) and language-specific (French). Generalization to larger or more diverse corpora and other languages is not demonstrated. - Parameter selection and reporting: the procedure for selecting α, β, γ and the final chosen configuration is only partially described; tables and text sometimes appear to omit exact reported parameter values. A clearer account of model selection (e.g., on validation splits) would help reproducibility. - LDA for keyword extraction: using LDA configured to one topic per cluster is reasonable given similar sentences, but details (number of iterations, priors, vocabulary preprocessing) are missing; LDA may be unstable on such small inputs. - Statistical testing and significance: the paper claims statistical superiority for informativity but does not report p-values, test types, or effect sizes. Provide more rigorous significance testing. - Ablation analysis: while the argument is that cohesion + keywords + 3-grams improves quality, the paper would benefit from ablation experiments that isolate the contribution of each component (e.g., cohesion only, cohesion+keywords, cohesion+3grams). - Runtime and scalability: although each compression is solved in under 6 seconds on modest hardware, there is no discussion of worst-case behavior, solver settings, or how the approach scales with larger graphs (more sentences, larger vocabularies). - Presentation issues: a few repetitions and minor editorial issues in the text; some tables referenced in the manuscript seem partially truncated in the provided extract. Recommendations for improvement: - Add a detailed description of the LDA setup and of the exact procedure used to tune α/β/γ (including validation set, search procedure). - Include ablation studies and report statistical tests (with p-values) for automatic and human evaluations. - Report more complete runtime/solver configuration details and discuss scalability. - Make code and data splits available to increase reproducibility. Overall assessment: the paper presents a solid, well-motivated incremental contribution to MSC by integrating combinatorial optimization and additional informativeness signals into a formal ILP. The experimental results are promising though somewhat limited in scope. With clarifications and additional experiments (ablation, significance, scalability), this work would be of interest to the summarization and NLP communities.
